Definition

Heart failure is a clinical syndrome characterised by typical symptoms (dyspnoea, ankle swelling, fatigue) caused by structural and/or functional cardiac abnormality, resulting in reduced cardiac output and/or elevated intracardiac pressures at rest or during stress. Requires objective evidence of cardiac dysfunction.

⭐ High-Yield Pearls
  • HF is a clinical syndrome, not a diagnosis — always identify the aetiology
  • EF cutoff: HFrEF <40%, HFmrEF 40–49%, HFpEF ≥50%
  • BNP >100 pg/mL or NT-proBNP >300 pg/mL supports diagnosis
  • ACEi/ARB + beta-blocker + MRA = proven mortality benefit in HFrEF
  • SGLT2 inhibitors (dapagliflozin, empagliflozin) reduce hospitalisation in both HFrEF and HFpEF

Precipitants of Acute Decompensation (FAILURE mnemonic)
  • Forgetting medications / non-compliance
  • Arrhythmia (AF most common)
  • Ischaemia / infarction
  • Life-threatening infection / sepsis
  • Up in sodium/fluid intake
  • Renal failure (cardiorenal syndrome)
  • Embolism (PE), Endocarditis, Exacerbation of COPD

Demographics
  • Age: Prevalence rises steeply with age — <1% under 55, ~10% over 75
  • Sex: HFrEF commoner in men; HFpEF commoner in women (oestrogen-related differences in LV remodelling)
  • Race: Black patients have higher incidence, younger age of onset, and higher rates of hypertensive HF
  • Trend: Rising prevalence due to ageing population and improved survival after MI

Framingham Risk Criteria (original HF risk)
  • Hypertension accounts for 39% of HF in men and 59% in women (Framingham data)
  • Diabetes independently doubles HF risk regardless of CAD
  • Obesity confers 34% increased risk per 1-unit BMI rise above 30
  • Atrial fibrillation triples HF risk and is both a cause and consequence

sections.title: Pathophysiology
sections.label: Pathophysiology
sections.sub: Neurohormonal activation · Cardiac remodelling · Haemodynamic consequences
sections.html:
1
Index Event: Myocardial injury (MI, pressure overload, toxin) → reduced contractility or compliance
2
Reduced CO: Baroreceptors detect ↓ arterial pressure → activate SNS and RAAS
3
SNS Activation: ↑ Noradrenaline → tachycardia, vasoconstriction, ↑ preload/afterload; chronic β1 downregulation
4
RAAS Activation: ↓ Renal perfusion → renin → angiotensin II → aldosterone → Na⁺/H₂O retention → ↑ preload
5
ADH Release: Posterior pituitary → ↑ water retention → hyponatraemia in severe HF
6
Cardiac Remodelling: AngII + aldosterone → myocyte hypertrophy, fibrosis, apoptosis → ventricular dilation
7
Counterregulatory: ANP/BNP released from stretched myocytes → natriuresis, vasodilation (overwhelmed in severe HF)
8
Vicious Cycle: Remodelling → ↑ wall stress → ↑ O₂ demand → further dysfunction → decompensation
Key Molecular Mechanisms
  • β1-adrenoceptor downregulation: Chronic SNS activation causes receptor internalisation — explains why acute catecholamines less effective in end-stage HF
  • Fetal gene programme: Pressure overload re-activates fetal contractile proteins (β-MHC, ANF) — less efficient, more energy-demanding
  • Calcium handling: ↓ SERCA2a activity → impaired Ca²⁺ re-uptake → diastolic dysfunction and arrhythmias
  • Nitric oxide pathway: Shear stress → eNOS → NO → cGMP → vasodilation; impaired in HF
Frank-Starling Mechanism

Within physiological limits, increased preload (ventricular filling) increases stroke volume. In HF, the curve is depressed and flattened — same preload produces less CO, and further volume loading causes pulmonary oedema without CO improvement.

sections.title: Clinical
sections.label: Clinical Features
sections.sub: Framingham criteria · ESC diagnostic algorithm · NICE CG108
sections.html:
⚡ Classic Presentation

Elderly patient with progressive dyspnoea on exertion, orthopnoea (2–3 pillow), paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea, bilateral ankle swelling, and fatigue. Examination: ↑JVP, bibasal crackles, S3 gallop, peripheral oedema.

Symptoms
  • Dyspnoea on exertion: Most common symptom — reduced CO + pulmonary venous hypertension
  • Orthopnoea: Dyspnoea lying flat — redistribution of fluid from legs to thorax; quantify by number of pillows
  • PND: Woken 1–2 hours after sleep gasping; relieved by sitting up
  • Fatigue: ↓ CO to skeletal muscle + anaemia + deconditioning
  • Ankle oedema: Bilateral, pitting, worse at end of day
  • Reduced exercise tolerance
  • Nocturnal cough: Especially with ACEi — but also pulmonary oedema
  • Abdominal bloating/early satiety: Hepatomegaly + gut oedema in RHF
  • Cardiac cachexia: Late feature — poor prognosis
Signs
  • ↑JVP: Elevated venous pressure — best indicator of volume overload
  • Bibasal crackles: Fine inspiratory; pulmonary oedema (not cleared by coughing)
  • S3 gallop: Highly specific for HF — ventricular filling sound in early diastole
  • Tachycardia: Compensatory; poor prognostic sign
  • Displaced apex beat: Lateral displacement — cardiomegaly
  • Peripheral oedema: Pitting, bilateral; can be absent in chronic compensated HF
  • Hepatomegaly: Tender; hepatojugular reflux positive in RHF
  • Ascites: Severe/longstanding RHF
  • Cheyne-Stokes breathing: Severe HF — periodic hyperventilation alternating with apnoea
  • Wheeze: 'Cardiac asthma' — bronchospasm from pulmonary oedema
Killip Classification (post-MI HF)
ClassFeatures30-Day Mortality
INo HF signs~6%
IIS3, crackles <50% lung fields, or ↑JVP~17%
IIIPulmonary oedema — crackles >50% lung fields~38%
IVCardiogenic shock (SBP <90, oliguria, cyanosis)~81%

sections.html:
Diagnostic Algorithm (ESC 2021)

Step 1: Clinical assessment (symptoms, signs, risk factors) → Step 2: ECG + BNP/NT-proBNP → if abnormal → Step 3: Echocardiogram (gold standard for EF + structure) → Step 4: Identify aetiology (stress test, CMR, coronary angiography as indicated)

TestKey Finding in HFSensitivity/SpecificityRelevance
BNP>100 pg/mL (acute: >400 rule-in); <35 pg/mL rules outSn 90%, Sp 76%First-line biomarker; guides diuresis; prognostic
NT-proBNP>300 pg/mL (acute); age-adjusted: <125 pg/mL rules outSn 90%, Sp 84%Longer half-life; better in CKD patients
ECGLVH, LBBB, AF, Q waves, ST changesSn 89% (abnormal ECG)Rules out HF if completely normal; identifies aetiology
CXRCardiomegaly (CTR >0.5), Kerley B lines, upper lobe diversion, bat-wing oedema, pleural effusionSn 68%, Sp 82%Rapid bedside assessment; acute pulmonary oedema
EchocardiogramEF, wall motion, LV size, valve function, diastolic parameters (E/e' ratio), LVOT VTIGold standardEssential for all HF — defines type and aetiology
FBCAnaemia (precipitant/complication); eosinophilia (eosinophilic myocarditis)—Treat reversible causes
U&E / CreatinineAKI (cardiorenal), hypokalaemia (diuretics), hyponatraemia (severe HF)—Guide diuretic therapy; monitor RAAS blockers
LFTs↑AST/ALT/bilirubin — congestive hepatopathy in RHF—Assess hepatic congestion severity
TFTsHypothyroidism (↓CO), hyperthyroidism (AF, high-output HF)—Reversible cause
TroponinElevated in acute decompensation (demand ischaemia)—Prognostic; rule out ACS precipitant
Cardiac MRILate gadolinium enhancement pattern — ischaemic vs non-ischaemic; myocarditis, amyloid, sarcoid, ARVCGold standard for aetiologyWhen echo inconclusive or cardiomyopathy suspected
Coronary AngiographyCoronary anatomy; identifies ischaemic aetiology—All new HFrEF patients; before LVAD/transplant listing
Cardiopulmonary Exercise Test (CPET)VO₂max <14 mL/kg/min = transplant consideration—Transplant/LVAD evaluation; prognosis

sections.title: Diagnosis
sections.label: Gold Standard Diagnosis
sections.sub: Framingham Criteria · ESC 2021 · Boston Criteria
sections.html:
ESC 2021 Diagnostic Requirements (ALL 3 required)
  • 1. Symptoms typical of HF (dyspnoea, fatigue, ankle swelling)
  • 2. Signs typical of HF (↑JVP, pulmonary crackles, peripheral oedema) — may be absent in compensated HF
  • 3. Objective evidence of structural/functional cardiac abnormality (echo EF, elevated natriuretics, abnormal diastolic parameters)
Framingham Criteria (requires 2 major OR 1 major + 2 minor)
Major CriteriaMinor Criteria
Paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoeaBilateral ankle oedema
Neck vein distensionNocturnal cough
Rales (crackles)Dyspnoea on ordinary exertion
Cardiomegaly on CXRHepatomegaly
Acute pulmonary oedemaPleural effusion
S3 gallopTachycardia (HR >120 bpm)
Elevated venous pressure (>16 cmH₂O)Reduction in VC by 1/3 from maximum
Hepatojugular reflux—
Weight loss >4.5 kg in 5 days in response to treatment—
Cardiothoracic Ratio (CXR)
CTR = Maximum cardiac diameter / Maximum thoracic diameter > 0.5 = cardiomegaly
BNP Rule-Out (ESC 2021)
Acute setting: BNP <100 pg/mL OR NT-proBNP <300 pg/mL → HF very unlikely (NPV >98%)
Diastolic Dysfunction — E/e' Ratio (Echo)
E/e' >15 = elevated LV filling pressures; E/e' 8–14 = intermediate (use additional parameters); E/e' <8 = normal filling

sections.title: Management
sections.label: Medical Management
sections.sub: ESC 2021 · ACC/AHA 2022 · NICE NG106 · PARADIGM-HF · DAPA-HF · EMPEROR-Reduced
sections.html:
Goals of Treatment
  • Reduce mortality and hospitalisation (ACEi/ARB, beta-blocker, MRA, SGLT2i, ARNI)
  • Relieve symptoms and improve exercise capacity (diuretics, nitrates)
  • Prevent disease progression and hospitalisation
  • Treat underlying aetiology and precipitants
Acute Decompensated HF (ADHF) Management
1
Position: Sit upright (reduces preload); high-flow O₂ if SpO₂ <94%
2
IV Furosemide: 40–80mg IV bolus (or double oral dose); titrate to urine output; monitor U&E daily
3
NIV (CPAP): For APO not responding to O₂/diuretics — reduces intubation rate (3CPAP trial)
4
Nitrates: GTN sublingual or IV infusion if hypertensive — venodilation reduces preload; avoid if SBP <90
5
Monitor: Urine output (target >0.5 mL/kg/hr), daily weight, U&E, BP
6
Cardiogenic shock: Inotropes (dobutamine) or vasopressors (noradrenaline); consider IABP/Impella
Chronic HFrEF — Guideline-Directed Medical Therapy (GDMT)
Ramipril (ACEi)
ACE Inhibitor — RAAS blockade
MOA: Blocks conversion of AngI→AngII; ↓ vasoconstriction, aldosterone, preload/afterload, cardiac remodelling | SE: Dry cough (10–15%), hyperkalaemia, AKI, angioedema | CI: Bilateral RAS, pregnancy, history of angioedema
Start: 1.25–2.5mg BD → Target: 5–10mg BD (titrate over weeks)
Monitor
U&E, Cr, BP at 1–2 weeks
Trials
CONSENSUS 1987, SOLVD 1991
Tier
First-line HFrEF
Carvedilol / Bisoprolol / Metoprolol succinate
Beta-blocker — Neurohumoral blockade
MOA: β1 blockade → ↓ HR, ↓ myocardial O₂ demand, ↓ arrhythmia, reverse remodelling; carvedilol also α1 | SE: Bradycardia, fatigue, cold peripheries, bronchospasm | CI: Acute decompensation, cardiogenic shock, severe asthma, bradycardia <50
Bisoprolol: Start 1.25mg OD → Target 10mg OD; Carvedilol: Start 3.125mg BD → Target 25mg BD
Monitor
HR, BP, symptoms
Trials
MERIT-HF, COPERNICUS, CIBIS-II
Tier
First-line HFrEF (start when stable)
Spironolactone / Eplerenone
Mineralocorticoid Receptor Antagonist (MRA)
MOA: Competitive aldosterone antagonism at renal tubule → ↓ Na⁺ retention, ↓ cardiac fibrosis and remodelling | SE: Hyperkalaemia, AKI, gynaecomastia (spironolactone), breast pain | CI: K⁺ >5.0, Cr >220 μmol/L, eGFR <30
Spironolactone: 25–50mg OD; Eplerenone: 25–50mg OD
Monitor
K⁺, Cr at 1 week then monthly
Trials
RALES 1999 (spiro), EPHESUS 2003 (epler)
Tier
First-line; add to ACEi+BB if EF≤35%
Sacubitril/Valsartan (Entresto)
ARNI — Angiotensin Receptor–Neprilysin Inhibitor
MOA: Valsartan blocks AT1R; sacubitril inhibits neprilysin → ↑ BNP/ANP → ↑ natriuresis, vasodilation, anti-fibrotic | SE: Hypotension, hyperkalaemia, AKI, angioedema (higher risk if ACEi within 36h) | CI: Prior angioedema with ACEi/ARB, concomitant ACEi (washout 36h), pregnancy
Start: 24/26mg BD → Target: 97/103mg BD (PARADIGM-HF target dose)
Monitor
BP, K⁺, Cr; BNP unreliable (neprilysin inhibited)
Trials
PARADIGM-HF 2014 (20% ↓ CV death vs enalapril)
Tier
Replace ACEi/ARB if EF≤40% + symptomatic
Dapagliflozin / Empagliflozin
SGLT2 Inhibitor — Sodium-glucose cotransporter-2 inhibitor
MOA: Inhibit SGLT2 in PCT → glycosuria, natriuresis, ↓ preload/afterload, ↑ haematopoiesis, myocardial energetics shift; mechanism in HF partially independent of glucose-lowering | SE: Genital mycotic infections, DKA (rare, especially T1DM), UTI, volume depletion | CI: eGFR <20 (dapagliflozin), T1DM (relative), recurrent UTI
Dapagliflozin: 10mg OD; Empagliflozin: 10mg OD (no titration required)
Monitor
eGFR, HbA1c, BP, genital symptoms
Trials
DAPA-HF 2019, EMPEROR-Reduced 2020, EMPEROR-Preserved 2021
Tier
First-line; benefit in HFrEF AND HFpEF
Furosemide / Bumetanide
Loop Diuretic — Symptom relief
MOA: Inhibits Na⁺-K⁺-2Cl⁻ cotransporter in thick ascending limb → natriuresis, diuresis, venodilation | SE: Hypokalaemia, hyponatraemia, hypomagnesaemia, ototoxicity (high IV dose), gout, AKI | CI: Anuria; caution in severe hypovolaemia
Furosemide: 20–40mg OD PO → up to 250mg/day; IV: 40–80mg bolus in ADHF; bumetanide 1mg = furosemide 40mg
Monitor
Daily weight, U&E, urine output
Trials
DOSE trial (high vs low dose strategy)
Tier
Symptom relief — no mortality benefit
Device Therapy
DeviceIndicationBenefit
ICDEF ≤35% + NYHA II–III despite 3+ months GDMT; primary prevention SCD↓ SCD by 23–55% (MADIT-II, SCD-HeFT)
CRT-P / CRT-DEF ≤35% + LBBB + QRS ≥150ms + NYHA II–IV (sinus rhythm)↑ EF, ↓ symptoms, ↓ mortality (CARE-HF, COMPANION)
LVADStage D HF — bridge to transplant or destination therapy; NYHA IIIb–IV↑ survival vs medical therapy (REMATCH trial)
Cardiac TransplantStage D, VO₂max <12–14 mL/kg/min, no contraindications5-year survival ~70%; limited by donor availability

sections.title: Special Regimens
sections.label: MDR / Special Regimens
sections.sub: HFpEF · Cardiac amyloidosis · Peripartum · Refractory HF
sections.html:
HFpEF Management (EF ≥50%)
HFpEF — No Proven Mortality Benefit Until SGLT2i Era
  • Diuretics: For congestion/symptom relief — furosemide/bumetanide
  • SGLT2 inhibitors: Empagliflozin/dapagliflozin — EMPEROR-Preserved and DELIVER trials showed ↓ HF hospitalisations (first drug class with proven HFpEF benefit)
  • BP control: Target <130/80 mmHg — RAAS blockers, calcium channel blockers
  • AF rate control: Beta-blockers, digoxin, verapamil
  • Treat underlying cause: Weight loss in obesity (STEP-HFpEF trial — semaglutide ↓ symptoms), revascularisation if ischaemic, valve repair
Cardiac Amyloidosis (ATTR) — Emerging Therapies
Tafamidis (Vyndamax/Vyndaqel)
TTR stabiliser — Transthyretin amyloidosis
MOA: Stabilises transthyretin tetramer → prevents misfolding and amyloid fibril deposition in myocardium | SE: Generally well-tolerated; mild GI effects | CI: Not for AL amyloidosis (different pathology)
Tafamidis meglumine 80mg OD OR tafamidis 61mg OD
Monitor
Echo, NT-proBNP, eGFR
Trials
ATTR-ACT 2018 (30% ↓ mortality, 32% ↓ hospitalisation)
Tier
First-line ATTR-CM
Peripartum Cardiomyopathy
⚡ Management Pearls
  • Onset: Last month of pregnancy to 5 months postpartum
  • Bromocriptine (dopamine agonist): Inhibits prolactin → ↓ cleaved prolactin fragment → prevents peripartum CM; 2.5mg BD × 2 weeks (supported by BOARD trial)
  • Hydralazine + nitrates: Used instead of ACEi/ARB during pregnancy (teratogenic)
  • ACEi/ARB/ARNI: Safe postpartum — start after delivery if not breastfeeding
  • EF often recovers in 50% by 6 months — reassess before subsequent pregnancy
PARADIGM-HF (2014)
Design: Double-blind RCT · N=8,442 · Duration: Median 27 months · Population: HFrEF EF≤40%, NYHA II–IV
Finding: Sacubitril/valsartan reduced CV death or HF hospitalisation by 20% vs enalapril (HR 0.80, p<0.001). Clinical impact: Replaced ACEi as preferred RAAS blockade in HFrEF; landmark change to HF guidelines.
DAPA-HF (2019)
Design: RCT · N=4,744 · Duration: Median 18.2 months · Population: HFrEF EF≤40%, NYHA II–IV, with/without T2DM
Finding: Dapagliflozin 10mg OD reduced worsening HF or CV death by 26% vs placebo (HR 0.74, p<0.001). Clinical impact: Established SGLT2i as 4th pillar of GDMT regardless of diabetes status.
EMPEROR-Preserved (2021)
Design: RCT · N=5,988 · Duration: Median 26 months · Population: HFpEF EF>40%, NT-proBNP elevated
Finding: Empagliflozin reduced HF hospitalisation or CV death by 21% vs placebo (HR 0.79, p<0.001). Clinical impact: First drug to show significant benefit in HFpEF — changed guideline recommendations.

Constrictive PericarditisKussmaul's sign, pericardial knock, pericardial calcification on CT, prominent x+y descent JVP↑JVP, ascites, peripheral oedema, fatigueCMR/CT (pericardial thickening); echo (septal bounce, respiratory variation); catheterisation (equalisation of diastolic pressures)
Hypoalbuminaemia (malnutrition)Bilateral oedema without dyspnoea, JVP not elevated, no S3, cachectic appearancePeripheral oedemaSerum albumin <25g/L; normal BNP; normal echo; dietary history
HypothyroidismDry skin, cold intolerance, constipation, periorbital oedema (non-pitting), bradycardia, ↑TSHFatigue, exertional dyspnoea, pericardial effusion possibleTFTs (↑TSH, ↓FT4); echo may show pericardial effusion; resolves with thyroxine
AnaemiaHigh-output state, pallor, koilonychia, tachycardia, hyperdynamic apex, flow murmurFatigue, dyspnoea, palpitationsFBC (Hb <8g/dL for significant symptoms); echo may show high CO; treat underlying cause

D); ARNI; spironolactone; warfarin in 1st trimester; amiodarone
Diabetes MellitusHF risk 2× higher; diabetic cardiomyopathy (HFpEF); SGLT2i highly beneficial; ACEI/ARB nephroprotective; HbA1c target 7–8% (tight control may worsen outcomes in HF)SGLT2 inhibitors (proven HF mortality benefit regardless of DM); ACEi/ARB; beta-blocker; metformin (cautious in NYHA IV); GLP-1 agonists (semaglutide — STEP-HFpEF)Thiazolidinediones (pioglitazone — fluid retention, worsen HF); saxagliptin/alogliptin (DPP-4i — increased HF hospitalisations in SAVOR-TIMI, EXAMINE)
CKD / Renal ImpairmentCardiorenal syndrome; ↑ mortality in CKD+HF; loop diuretics may worsen AKI; ACEi/ARB cause acute ↑Cr (acceptable up to 30%) — do not stop unless >50% rise or K⁺>5.5; haemofiltration for diuretic resistanceFurosemide (higher doses needed in CKD); ACEi/ARB (renoprotective long-term); eplerenone (less gynaecomastia); dapagliflozin down to eGFR 25 (DAPA-CKD)NSAIDs; spironolactone if eGFR <30; digoxin at full dose (narrow TI, renally cleared — accumulates in CKD); contrast agents without pre-hydration
Elderly (>75 yrs)HFpEF commoner; polypharmacy risk; frailty; falls risk with diuretics/hypotension; cognitive impairment affects adherence; atypical presentations (fatigue, confusion rather than dyspnoea)Start GDMT at low doses and titrate slowly; diuretics for symptom relief; SGLT2i well-tolerated; geriatric assessment; cardiac rehab adapted for frailtyRoutine ICD implantation in frailty/limited life expectancy; aggressive diuresis (orthostatic hypotension); alpha-blockers (fall risk)
Paediatric / CongenitalHF secondary to CHD most common (VSD, AVSD, large PDA); cardiomyopathy (DCM — genetic); myocarditis; different pharmacokinetics; weight-based dosingCaptopril (paediatric ACEi); carvedilol; furosemide; digoxin (still used in paediatrics for rate control + inotropy); surgical correction of structural lesionsFull adult doses; spironolactone if neonatal (electrolyte monitoring essential); amiodarone (significant toxicity profile in children)

sections.title: Exam Pearls
sections.label: High-Yield Exam Pearls
sections.sub: USMLE Step 2 · MRCP · PLAB · Finals
sections.html:
1. BNP <35 pg/mL (chronic) or <100 pg/mL (acute) effectively RULES OUT heart failure (NPV >98%)
  2. S3 gallop is highly SPECIFIC for HF (>90%) but poorly sensitive — its presence is very significant
  3. ARNI (sacubitril/valsartan) must NOT be started within 36 hours of last ACEi dose due to angioedema risk
  4. SGLT2 inhibitors are the only drug class proven to reduce HF hospitalisations in BOTH HFrEF AND HFpEF
  5. Beta-blockers are CONTRAINDICATED in acute decompensated HF but are ESSENTIAL in stable chronic HFrEF
  6. Loop diuretics reduce symptoms but have NO mortality benefit in HF — do not mistake diuresis for disease modification
  7. Tafamidis is the treatment for ATTR cardiac amyloidosis — distinguish from AL amyloidosis (treat underlying plasma cell dyscrasia)
  8. CRT is indicated when EF ≤35% + LBBB + QRS ≥150ms + NYHA II–IV — LBBB morphology matters more than QRS duration alone
  9. Cardiogenic shock = Killip Class IV: SBP <90 mmHg, cool peripheries, oliguria — inotropes first, then consider mechanical support (IABP, Impella)
  10. Peripartum cardiomyopathy: bromocriptine inhibits prolactin → reduces cardiotoxic prolactin cleavage product; ACEi contraindicated during breastfeeding
  11. Hyponatraemia (Na⁺ <130) in HF = very poor prognostic sign; treat with fluid restriction and tolvaptan (V2 antagonist)
  12. Digoxin reduces HF hospitalisations (DIG trial) but does NOT reduce mortality — narrow therapeutic index (0.5–0.9 ng/mL for HF)
  13. Right heart catheterisation gold standard for haemodynamic assessment: PCWP >18 mmHg = cardiogenic pulmonary oedema
  14. Thiazolidinediones (pioglitazone/rosiglitazone) are CONTRAINDICATED in HF — cause fluid retention and worsen outcomes
  15. LVAD destination therapy: REMATCH trial showed survival benefit over optimal medical therapy in Stage D HF ineligible for transplant

sections.title: Exam Traps
sections.label: Exam Traps
sections.sub: Common mistakes in diagnosis, investigations, and management
sections.html:
⚠️
ACEi before ARNI trap: Students start sacubitril/valsartan (ARNI) immediately after ACEi. WRONG — must wait 36 hours between last ACEi dose and first ARNI dose to prevent angioedema (both elevate bradykinin via different mechanisms).
⚠️
Beta-blocker in ADHF trap: Beta-blockers are essential for chronic HFrEF but are CONTRAINDICATED in acute decompensated HF (cardiogenic shock, wet/cold state). Start only when patient is euvolaemic and stable — exam will test this distinction.
⚠️
BNP interpretation trap: BNP is elevated by HF, CKD, PE, AF, and sepsis. BNP can be falsely LOW in obesity (adipocytes clear BNP) and in very acute/flash APO (no time for BNP to rise). Don't exclude HF based on BNP alone if clinical picture is compelling.
⚠️
Spironolactone in CKD trap: MRAs are beneficial in HFrEF BUT are contraindicated when K⁺ >5.0 mEq/L or Cr >220 μmol/L (eGFR <30). Hyperkalaemia can be fatal — always check renal function before initiating and 1 week after starting.
⚠️
Thiazolidinedione trap: Pioglitazone/rosiglitazone worsen HF by causing sodium and water retention — CONTRAINDICATED in HF (any class). Exam may present a diabetic patient with worsening oedema after starting a new diabetes medication — think TZD.
⚠️
Digoxin monitoring trap: Digoxin target range for HF is 0.5–0.9 ng/mL (lower than the historical 1.0–2.0 range for AF rate control). Higher levels = MORE mortality. Toxicity risk ↑ with hypokalaemia, hypomagnesaemia, hypothyroidism, and CKD.
⚠️
HFpEF treatment trap: No ACEi/ARB/beta-blocker has proven mortality benefit in HFpEF (unlike HFrEF). Only SGLT2 inhibitors have proven to reduce hospitalisation in HFpEF (EMPEROR-Preserved, DELIVER). Diuretics are for symptom relief only.
⚠️
Peripartum CM + ACEi trap: ACEi/ARBs are teratogenic (Pregnancy Cat. D) and contraindicated during pregnancy AND breastfeeding. Use hydralazine + nitrates during pregnancy. Only switch to ACEi/ARB postpartum after confirmed cessation of breastfeeding.
⚠️
ICD in NYHA IV trap: ICD implantation is NOT routinely recommended in NYHA Class IV patients (unless bridge to transplant/LVAD), as most deaths are due to pump failure (not VT/VF). Primary prevention ICD indication is NYHA Class II–III with EF ≤35%.

sections.title: Prognosis
sections.label: Natural History & Prognosis
sections.h1: Prognosis & Natural History of Heart Failure
sections.sub: MAGGIC score · Seattle HF Model · ESC data · Framingham cohort
sections.html:
5-Year Mortality (all HF)
~50%
worse than many cancers
NYHA IV 1-Year Mortality
50–75%
without advanced Rx
30-Day Readmission
25%
post-discharge
LVAD 2-Year Survival
~60–70%
destination therapy
Transplant 5-Year Survival
~70%
with optimal matching
SCD Proportion
50%
of HF deaths
Good Prognostic Factors
  • Younger age at diagnosis
  • Non-ischaemic aetiology
  • EF >35–40% (or recovered — HFimpEF)
  • NYHA Class I–II on treatment
  • VO₂max >14 mL/kg/min
  • Normal renal function
  • Adherent to GDMT
  • CRT response (LBBB morphology)
  • Peripartum CM with EF recovery
Poor Prognostic Factors
  • EF <25%
  • Hyponatraemia (Na⁺ <130)
  • Elevated BNP/NT-proBNP
  • Renal impairment (CKD stage 3+)
  • NYHA Class III–IV
  • Cardiac cachexia
  • Repeated hospitalisations
  • Elevated troponin at rest
  • Wide QRS / LBBB without CRT
  • Pulsus alternans
  • Cheyne-Stokes respiration
Stage A
Risk factors only — no structural disease. 10-year risk of developing HF ~15–20% with multiple risk factors
Stage B
Asymptomatic LV dysfunction — structural disease present. Annual progression to symptomatic HF ~5–10%
Stage C
Symptomatic HF — annual mortality 10–15%; 5-year survival 50% despite GDMT; hospitalisations begin
Stage D
Refractory HF — 1-year mortality 50–75% without advanced therapy (LVAD/transplant). Palliative care discussion appropriate
MAGGIC Meta-Analysis (2012)
Design: Individual patient meta-analysis · N=39,372 · HFrEF + HFpEF · 30 cohorts
Finding: HFpEF mortality lower than HFrEF at 1 year (22% vs 26%) but equal at 5 years (~50%). Clinical impact: Established that HFpEF carries similar long-term mortality to HFrEF, challenging the 'benign' label.

quiz.q: A 68-year-old man with hypertension and type 2 diabetes presents with 3 months of progressive exertional dyspnoea and bilateral ankle swelling. He sleeps on 3 pillows. Examination reveals JVP elevated 5 cm above sternal angle, bibasal fine crackles, a third heart sound, and 2+ pitting oedema to the knees. ECG shows LBBB with QRS 155ms. Echocardiogram shows EF 28%, dilated LV with global hypokinesia. NT-proBNP is 4,200 pg/mL. Which medication combination has the strongest evidence for reducing all-cause mortality in this patient?
opts: A. Furosemide + digoxin + hydralazine/nitrate
B. Sacubitril/valsartan + bisoprolol + spironolactone + dapagliflozin
C. Ramipril + bisoprolol + spironolactone
D. Furosemide + amlodipine + ramipril
E. Ivabradine + ramipril + furosemide
quiz.exp: The patient has HFrEF (EF 28%, NYHA Class III). Current ESC 2021 and ACC/AHA 2022 guidelines recommend a 'fantastic four' of GDMT for HFrEF: ARNI (sacubitril/valsartan) — PARADIGM-HF showed 20% reduction in CV death/HF hospitalisation vs enalapril; beta-blocker (bisoprolol) — CIBIS-II showed 34% mortality reduction; MRA (spironolactone) — RALES showed 30% mortality reduction; SGLT2 inhibitor (dapagliflozin) — DAPA-HF showed 26% reduction in worsening HF/CV death. This four-drug combination is now the evidence-based standard for HFrEF.
whys: Furosemide + digoxin + hydralazine/nitrate: digoxin reduces hospitalisations (DIG trial) but NOT mortality; hydralazine/nitrate is an alternative when ACEi/ARNi are not tolerated (A-HeFT trial — particularly in Black patients) but is inferior to RAAS blockade; furosemide provides symptom relief only. This combination lacks the full GDMT evidence base.

Ramipril + bisoprolol + spironolactone: This was the correct pre-2014 regimen but is now superseded. PARADIGM-HF (2014) demonstrated sacubitril/valsartan superiority over enalapril (HR 0.80, p<0.001), and SGLT2 inhibitors have since been added as the 4th pillar. Continuing ACEi alone when ARNI is tolerated and indicated represents suboptimal therapy.

Furosemide + amlodipine + ramipril: Amlodipine has neutral mortality effect in HF (PRAISE-1/2 trials — no benefit, no harm in non-ischaemic DCM). This regimen lacks proven mortality-reducing drugs (beta-blocker, MRA, SGLT2i). Calcium channel blockers (especially rate-limiting types like verapamil/diltiazem) can worsen HF and are generally avoided in HFrEF.

Ivabradine + ramipril + furosemide: Ivabradine (SHIFT trial) reduces HF hospitalisation when HR >70 bpm in sinus rhythm already on maximum tolerated beta-blocker, but is an ADD-ON therapy, not a first-line agent. This regimen omits beta-blocker, MRA, and SGLT2i — all of which have stronger mortality data than ivabradine.

quiz.q: A 55-year-old woman with no past cardiac history presents 6 weeks postpartum with worsening dyspnoea and bilateral leg oedema. She is currently breastfeeding. Echocardiogram shows EF 32% with a globally dilated LV. ECG shows sinus tachycardia at 105 bpm. NT-proBNP is 3,800 pg/mL. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial pharmacological management?
opts: A. Enalapril + carvedilol + furosemide
B. Hydralazine + carvedilol + furosemide + bromocriptine
C. Sacubitril/valsartan + furosemide + bisoprolol
D. Furosemide + digoxin only
E. Enalapril + furosemide + bromocriptine
quiz.exp: This is peripartum cardiomyopathy (PPCM). Key management points: (1) ACEi/ARBs are contraindicated during breastfeeding — they cross into breast milk and cause neonatal hypotension and renal failure; (2) Hydralazine + nitrates are the safe vasodilator alternative; (3) Beta-blockers (carvedilol — has some α1 blockade providing additional vasodilation) are safe in breastfeeding; (4) Bromocriptine — a dopamine agonist that inhibits prolactin — is recommended in PPCM as elevated prolactin is cleaved into a 16kDa fragment that causes cardiomyocyte apoptosis and vasoconstriction; the BOARD trial supported its use (2.5mg BD for 2 weeks, then 2.5mg OD for 6 weeks). Furosemide is used for decongestion.
whys: Enalapril + carvedilol + furosemide: Enalapril (ACEi) is contraindicated in breastfeeding — crosses into breast milk causing neonatal hypotension and renal impairment. While correct postpartum after weaning, it cannot be used while the patient is still breastfeeding. Bromocriptine is also omitted from this regimen.

Sacubitril/valsartan + furosemide + bisoprolol: ARNI contains valsartan (ARB component) — contraindicated in breastfeeding for the same reasons as ACEi. Also, ARNI must not be started within 36 hours of any ACEi dose. Bromocriptine is omitted.

Furosemide + digoxin only: Grossly inadequate. Digoxin has no mortality benefit and narrow therapeutic index. This regimen fails to address neurohormonal activation (no RAAS/SNS blockade), fails to address the prolactin-mediated mechanism (no bromocriptine), and provides only symptom relief. Inadequate for a patient with EF 32%.

Enalapril + furosemide + bromocriptine: While bromocriptine is correctly included, enalapril (ACEi) remains contraindicated in breastfeeding. The vasodilator must be hydralazine (with or without nitrate) until breastfeeding is completed. Carvedilol (beta-blocker) is also omitted from this regimen.

quiz.q: A 72-year-old man with known HFrEF (EF 30%) on optimal medical therapy presents with worsening dyspnoea over 48 hours. BP is 85/60 mmHg, HR 120 bpm, cool peripheries, and urine output 15 mL/hr. JVP is elevated and lungs have bilateral crackles. His current medications include bisoprolol 10mg, sacubitril/valsartan 97/103mg, spironolactone 25mg, and dapagliflozin 10mg. What is the most appropriate immediate management?
opts: A. IV furosemide 80mg stat and increase oral bisoprolol
B. Stop bisoprolol, withhold sacubitril/valsartan and spironolactone, start IV dobutamine, and cautious IV furosemide
C. IV furosemide 80mg + IV GTN infusion
D. Start IV noradrenaline immediately and give 500mL IV fluid challenge
E. Urgent CRT implantation
quiz.exp: This patient is in cardiogenic shock (Killip Class IV) — low output (hypotension SBP 85, oliguria) with fluid overload (elevated JVP, bilateral crackles). Management priorities: (1) Stop bisoprolol — negative inotropy worsens cardiogenic shock; do NOT increase it; (2) Withhold sacubitril/valsartan and spironolactone — vasodilation and hyperkalaemia risk in shock; (3) IV dobutamine — positive inotrope (β1 agonist) to increase CO and tissue perfusion in low-output state; (4) Cautious IV furosemide — for decongestion, but titrate carefully as excessive diuresis worsens preload in a patient already hypotensive. Noradrenaline added if dopamine/dobutamine insufficient. This is the ESC/ACC approach to cardiogenic shock complicating HF.
whys: IV furosemide 80mg stat and increase oral bisoprolol: Bisoprolol must be STOPPED (or at minimum withheld) in cardiogenic shock — it reduces HR and contractility, worsening already compromised cardiac output. Increasing bisoprolol in this context is dangerous and could precipitate cardiac arrest.

IV furosemide + IV GTN infusion: GTN is a vasodilator appropriate for acute pulmonary oedema WITH adequate blood pressure (SBP >90). In cardiogenic shock with SBP 85 mmHg, GTN is CONTRAINDICATED — it would further reduce preload/afterload and precipitate cardiovascular collapse. No inotropic support provided.

Start IV noradrenaline immediately and give 500mL IV fluid challenge: Noradrenaline (vasopressor) is appropriate if CO remains inadequate after dobutamine, but fluid challenge is DANGEROUS in cardiogenic shock — the patient already has elevated JVP and bilateral crackles (fluid overload). Fluid loading would worsen pulmonary oedema without improving CO (Frank-Starling curve is already on the flat/descending portion).

Urgent CRT implantation: CRT is indicated for STABLE HFrEF with LBBB + QRS ≥150ms + EF ≤35% + NYHA II–IV — it is an elective device procedure for chronic management, NOT an acute intervention for cardiogenic shock. Implanting a device in an acutely shocked patient carries prohibitive procedural risk.

quiz.q: A 64-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes and obesity (BMI 38) is found to have an EF of 55% on echocardiography requested for dyspnoea on exertion. Diastolic dysfunction is confirmed with E/e' ratio of 17. NT-proBNP is 580 pg/mL. She has no significant coronary artery disease on CT angiography. Medications include metformin and amlodipine. Which drug is most likely to reduce her risk of hospitalisation for heart failure?
opts: A. Ramipril
B. Bisoprolol
C. Spironolactone
D. Empagliflozin
E. Ivabradine
quiz.exp: This patient has HFpEF (EF 55%, diastolic dysfunction, elevated NT-proBNP) in the context of obesity and diabetes. The EMPEROR-Preserved trial (2021) demonstrated that empagliflozin 10mg OD reduced the risk of CV death or HF hospitalisation by 21% (HR 0.79, p<0.001) in patients with HFpEF (EF >40%). The DELIVER trial (dapagliflozin) confirmed these findings. SGLT2 inhibitors are currently the only drug class with a proven reduction in HF hospitalisation in HFpEF. ACEi, beta-blockers, MRA, and ivabradine do not have proven mortality or morbidity benefit in HFpEF based on current RCT evidence.
whys: Ramipril: The PEP-CHF trial (perindopril in HFpEF) and CHARM-Preserved (candesartan) showed modest reductions in HF hospitalisation but did not reach statistical significance. ACEi/ARBs are used for BP control in HFpEF but are NOT proven to reduce HF hospitalisations with the same level of evidence as SGLT2 inhibitors. Not the best answer.

Bisoprolol: No landmark RCT has demonstrated mortality or hospitalisation benefit for beta-blockers in HFpEF. Beta-blockers may even worsen exercise intolerance in HFpEF by blunting the chronotropic response. They are used for rate control in AF-related HFpEF but not for primary HFpEF morbidity reduction.

Spironolactone: The TOPCAT trial (spironolactone in HFpEF) showed non-significant results in the overall population (HR 0.89, p=0.14). A post-hoc analysis of Americas-enrolled patients suggested possible benefit, but this is hypothesis-generating only. Spironolactone is not guideline-recommended as a primary HFpEF therapy (used for symptom control in select patients).

Ivabradine: The EDIFY trial (ivabradine in HFpEF) showed no benefit on diastolic function, NT-proBNP, or 6-minute walk test. Ivabradine is indicated in HFrEF with HR >70 bpm in sinus rhythm on maximum beta-blocker, not in HFpEF. It has no evidence base in HFpEF management.

quiz.q: A 58-year-old man with ischaemic HFrEF (EF 32%) is on ramipril 10mg, carvedilol 25mg BD, spironolactone 25mg, and furosemide 80mg OD. He remains NYHA Class III. ECG shows sinus rhythm with LBBB, QRS duration 160ms, HR 68 bpm. Echocardiogram confirms EF 32% with no significant valvular disease. What is the most appropriate next step?
opts: A. Add digoxin 125 mcg OD
B. Switch ramipril to sacubitril/valsartan and refer for CRT implantation
C. Add ivabradine 5mg BD
D. Increase furosemide to 160mg OD
E. Refer directly for cardiac transplantation
quiz.exp: Two important upgrades are indicated: (1) Switch ramipril to sacubitril/valsartan (ARNI) — PARADIGM-HF showed 20% relative risk reduction in CV death/HF hospitalisation vs enalapril; ESC 2021 guidelines recommend ARNI over ACEi in symptomatic HFrEF tolerating RAAS blockade (remember: washout 36 hours between last ACEi dose and first ARNI dose); (2) CRT implantation — this patient meets all 3 criteria: EF ≤35%, LBBB morphology, QRS ≥150ms (QRS 160ms here), NYHA II–III in sinus rhythm. CARE-HF and COMPANION trials demonstrated mortality and HF hospitalisation benefit. CRT can improve EF by 10–15% in responders (super-responders may normalise EF).
whys: Add digoxin 125 mcg OD: Digoxin (DIG trial 1997) reduces HF hospitalisations but does NOT reduce all-cause mortality. It has a narrow therapeutic index (target 0.5–0.9 ng/mL in HF), toxicity risk (especially in CKD, hypokalaemia), and should be used as 3rd-line for symptom control or rate control in AF — not as an upgrade step when ARNI and CRT are indicated.

Add ivabradine 5mg BD: HR is 68 bpm — ivabradine is only indicated when HR >70 bpm in sinus rhythm on maximum tolerated beta-blocker. At HR 68, ivabradine is NOT indicated (risk of symptomatic bradycardia). Also, upgrading to ARNI and CRT should be prioritised before adding 5th-line agents.

Increase furosemide to 160mg OD: Furosemide provides symptom relief but no mortality benefit. Doubling the diuretic dose when there are proven disease-modifying interventions not yet optimised (ARNI upgrade, CRT) is the wrong priority. High-dose loop diuretics risk AKI, electrolyte disturbance, and neurohormonal activation.

Refer directly for cardiac transplantation: Transplant is Stage D (refractory HF) indication requiring VO₂max <12–14 mL/kg/min and failure of all GDMT including ARNI, CRT, and device therapy. This patient has not yet received ARNI or CRT — both of which could significantly improve EF and symptoms. Transplant listing at this stage is premature.

quiz.q: A 45-year-old man with newly diagnosed dilated cardiomyopathy (EF 22%) is started on carvedilol 3.125mg BD, ramipril 2.5mg BD, and spironolactone 25mg. Three weeks later, his serum potassium is 5.6 mEq/L and creatinine has risen from 90 to 130 μmol/L (eGFR 52 → 38 mL/min). He feels well and has no symptoms of uraemia. What is the most appropriate action?
opts: A. Stop all three medications immediately and start furosemide monotherapy
B. Stop spironolactone, halve ramipril dose, continue carvedilol, recheck U&E in 1–2 weeks
C. Continue all medications unchanged — these changes are expected and acceptable
D. Stop ramipril and switch to hydralazine/nitrate
E. Add patiromer and continue all medications unchanged
quiz.exp: This patient has hyperkalaemia (K⁺ 5.6 mEq/L — above 5.5 threshold) and significant AKI (Cr rise 90→130, eGFR 52→38) after starting RAAS blockade. Management: (1) Stop spironolactone — MRA contraindicated when K⁺ >5.0; it is the primary driver of hyperkalaemia here; (2) Halve ramipril dose — a >30% rise in creatinine warrants dose reduction (not immediate cessation unless >50% rise or K⁺ >5.5 with rising trend); (3) Continue carvedilol — beta-blocker has no direct effect on potassium or renal function at this stage; (4) Recheck U&E in 1–2 weeks — close monitoring after dose adjustment. Stopping all RAAS blockade (Option A) would be an overreaction and deprive the patient of mortality benefit from ACEi.
whys: Stop all three medications immediately and start furosemide monotherapy: This is excessive and harmful. A creatinine rise of up to 30–50% above baseline is acceptable and expected with ACEi initiation (reduced efferent arteriolar tone) and does not mandate immediate cessation. Stopping all GDMT denies proven mortality benefit. Furosemide monotherapy provides only symptom control.

Continue all medications unchanged: K⁺ 5.6 mEq/L exceeds the 5.0–5.5 threshold for continuing spironolactone safely. Continuing MRA with K⁺ 5.6 risks life-threatening hyperkalaemia (K⁺ >6.0 → arrhythmias, cardiac arrest). The creatinine rise (44% above baseline) also warrants dose adjustment. 'Continue unchanged' is unsafe.

Stop ramipril and switch to hydralazine/nitrate: Hydralazine/nitrate (A-HeFT trial) is an alternative ONLY when ACEi/ARB/ARNI are not tolerated at any dose due to renal failure, bilateral RAS, or angioedema. A dose reduction and monitoring period is appropriate before abandoning ACEi — it provides superior mortality benefit over hydralazine/nitrate in non-Black patients.

Add patiromer and continue all medications unchanged: Patiromer (K⁺ binder) can be used as an adjunct to allow continuation of RAAS blockers in CKD patients with tendency to hyperkalaemia (PEARL-HF trial concept), but the immediate priority when K⁺ is 5.6 and rising is to stop the primary offending agent (spironolactone). Adding patiromer without adjusting MRA dose delays the safer and simpler intervention.

quiz.q: A 66-year-old woman presents with sudden onset severe dyspnoea, frothy pink sputum, and diaphoresis. BP is 190/110 mmHg, HR 110 bpm, SpO₂ 84% on air. She has a history of hypertension but no known cardiac disease. CXR shows bilateral 'bat-wing' pulmonary oedema. ECG shows LVH with repolarisation changes. BNP is 1,800 pg/mL. What is the most appropriate immediate treatment?
opts: A. IV furosemide 80mg + IV morphine 2mg + supplemental O₂
B. Non-invasive ventilation (CPAP) + IV furosemide 40–80mg + IV GTN infusion
C. IV furosemide 250mg + immediate intubation
D. Oral furosemide 80mg + sublingual GTN + oral ramipril
E. IV noradrenaline infusion + high-flow O₂
quiz.exp: This is acute hypertensive pulmonary oedema (flash APO). The BP is 190/110 and SpO₂ 84% — this is a hypertensive emergency causing LV afterload mismatch. Management: (1) CPAP (non-invasive ventilation) — 3CPAP trial (Gray et al., 2008) showed CPAP reduces need for intubation, reduces dyspnoea and respiratory rate faster than standard therapy; indicated for SpO₂ <94% or RR >25 with APO; (2) IV furosemide — venodilation (early effect) + diuresis; (3) IV GTN infusion — potent venodilator reducing preload and afterload; particularly appropriate when BP >110 (as here, BP 190); GTN is the vasodilator of choice in hypertensive APO. Morphine (option A) is no longer routinely recommended in APO — associated with increased ICU admissions and mortality (ADHERE registry data).
whys: IV furosemide 80mg + IV morphine 2mg + supplemental O₂: Morphine is no longer recommended in acute pulmonary oedema. ADHERE registry data (2002) and subsequent analyses showed morphine use in ADHF was associated with increased mechanical ventilation, ICU admission, and in-hospital mortality. Current ESC guidelines (2021) advise AGAINST routine morphine use in APO. CPAP is omitted despite SpO₂ 84%.

IV furosemide 250mg + immediate intubation: 250mg furosemide is excessive as initial therapy. Immediate intubation should be reserved for failure of NIV, respiratory arrest, or haemodynamic collapse — not as first-line in a patient who is conscious and maintaining airway. CPAP should be tried first (evidence-based, non-invasive). Intubation carries procedural risk and ICU admission.

Oral furosemide 80mg + sublingual GTN + oral ramipril: Oral medications have unreliable absorption in acute cardiogenic pulmonary oedema (reduced gut perfusion). Sublingual GTN provides only brief effect (5–10 min). Ramipril (ACEi) is appropriate for long-term management but should NOT be started acutely in haemodynamically uncertain situations. CPAP is the priority here.

IV noradrenaline infusion + high-flow O₂: Noradrenaline is a vasopressor/inotrope used in CARDIOGENIC SHOCK (low BP, low CO state). This patient has BP 190/110 — the opposite problem (hypertensive emergency with high afterload). Noradrenaline would dramatically worsen LV afterload and pulmonary oedema. This is the wrong vasoactive agent.

quiz.q: A 70-year-old man with HFrEF (EF 28%) presents for routine follow-up. He is on sacubitril/valsartan 97/103mg, carvedilol 25mg BD, spironolactone 25mg, and dapagliflozin 10mg. He remains NYHA Class II with HR 76 bpm in sinus rhythm. Repeat echocardiography shows EF has improved to 42%. NT-proBNP is 210 pg/mL. What is the most appropriate management regarding his current HF medications?
opts: A. Stop all HF medications as EF has normalised
B. Stop spironolactone and SGLT2 inhibitor as EF is now >40%
C. Continue all four medications — this is HFimpEF and medications should not be stopped
D. Halve all medication doses as EF has improved
E. Stop sacubitril/valsartan and restart ramipril as EF is now >40%
quiz.exp: This patient has HFimpEF (Heart Failure with Improved Ejection Fraction) — previously EF <40%, now ≥40% on treatment. Critical point: medications should NOT be stopped. The TRED-HF trial (2019) demonstrated that in patients with recovered EF (dilated cardiomyopathy, EF improved to ≥50%), stepwise withdrawal of GDMT led to 44% relapse of LV dysfunction within 6 months (vs 0% in those who continued therapy). ESC 2021 guidelines state: in HFimpEF, medications should be continued indefinitely as EF recovery is often medication-dependent, not true myocardial recovery. Stopping therapy risks decompensation and re-remodelling.
whys: Stop all HF medications as EF has normalised: Critically wrong — TRED-HF trial showed 44% relapse of LV dysfunction within 6 months of stopping GDMT in patients with recovered EF. EF 'normalisation' on treatment does not mean the underlying cardiomyopathy has resolved. This is one of the most dangerous exam traps in HF management.

Stop spironolactone and SGLT2 inhibitor as EF is now >40%: While HFpEF evidence is stronger for EF ≥50%, the current EF of 42% (HFmrEF range) still represents residual dysfunction. More importantly, stopping any GDMT in HFimpEF risks relapse (TRED-HF). These medications should be maintained. The patient was originally HFrEF — the underlying substrate persists.

Halve all medication doses as EF has improved: There is no evidence base for dose reduction when EF improves. In fact, the improvements seen are partly due to optimal dosing of GDMT. Reducing doses could trigger re-remodelling. Target doses used in RCTs should be maintained where tolerated.

Stop sacubitril/valsartan and restart ramipril as EF is now >40%: ARNI superiority over ACEi was demonstrated across all EF ranges in follow-up analyses. There is no guideline recommendation to downgrade from ARNI to ACEi when EF improves. Switching back is an unnecessary step that removes proven superiority without benefit.

quiz.q: A 52-year-old man is admitted with palpitations and dyspnoea. Examination shows irregular pulse at 110 bpm, BP 105/70 mmHg, and bibasal crackles. ECG confirms AF with rapid ventricular response. Echo shows EF 35% and a globally hypokinetic but non-dilated LV — no wall motion abnormalities suggesting prior MI. He has had AF for approximately 6 months. His HF is attributed to the AF. After anticoagulation, what is the best rate/rhythm management strategy?
opts: A. Rate control with bisoprolol alone, accept chronic AF
B. Rate control with digoxin alone
C. Rhythm control with early cardioversion/catheter ablation aiming to restore sinus rhythm
D. Rate control with amiodarone plus bisoprolol
E. AV node ablation and permanent pacemaker implantation
quiz.exp: This is tachycardia-mediated cardiomyopathy (TMC) — AF for 6 months causing HF with EF 35%. The key insight is that this cardiomyopathy may be fully reversible if sinus rhythm is restored. The CASTLE-AF trial (2018) showed catheter ablation in HFrEF with AF significantly reduced the primary endpoint of death/HF hospitalisation vs rate control (HR 0.62, p=0.006) and improved EF by ~8% compared to rate control. More broadly, the EAST-AFNET 4 trial (2020) demonstrated early rhythm control reduces CV outcomes. In TMC specifically, restoring sinus rhythm can normalise EF — this is a potentially reversible cause that should be aggressively treated with rhythm control, not passive rate control.
whys: Rate control with bisoprolol alone, accept chronic AF: Rate control alone is suboptimal in tachycardia-mediated cardiomyopathy. If the underlying cause (persistent fast AF) is not addressed, myocardial dysfunction may persist or worsen despite rate control. CASTLE-AF showed rhythm control (ablation) was superior to rate control in HFrEF + AF. Accepting chronic AF without attempting rhythm control denies the patient potential EF recovery.

Rate control with digoxin alone: Digoxin provides rate control at rest but is less effective during exercise. More importantly, digoxin monotherapy does not address the rhythm problem causing the cardiomyopathy. Digoxin has no proven mortality benefit in HF and narrow therapeutic index — not appropriate as sole management for this potentially reversible condition.

Rate control with amiodarone plus bisoprolol: While bisoprolol for rate/rhythm control is reasonable, amiodarone for rate control alone in this context is suboptimal — amiodarone has significant long-term toxicity (thyroid, pulmonary, hepatic) and if rhythm control is the goal, catheter ablation has superior safety and efficacy in HFrEF (CASTLE-AF: 13% absolute reduction in primary endpoint vs medical therapy including amiodarone).

AV node ablation and permanent pacemaker implantation: AV node ablation with pacemaker creates permanent pacemaker-dependency and pacing-induced dyssynchrony — only appropriate when drug therapy fails and catheter ablation is not feasible. CRT-pacing may be needed post-AV node ablation in HFrEF. This is a last resort, not a first-line rhythm strategy in a patient where ablation/cardioversion has not been tried.

quiz.q: A 78-year-old man with Stage D HFrEF (EF 15%) has been hospitalised four times in the past year. VO₂max on CPET is 9.8 mL/kg/min. He is on maximum GDMT and has a biventricular ICD. He is now considered for advanced therapies. He has no significant comorbidities, is ambulatory, and has a good social support system. Serum creatinine is 180 μmol/L (eGFR 28). Which advanced therapy is most appropriate?
opts: A. Cardiac transplantation — list urgently
B. LVAD as destination therapy
C. Haemodiafiltration for cardiorenal syndrome
D. Continuous IV inotrope infusion as palliative bridging
E. Increase furosemide dose and add metolazone
quiz.exp: This patient has Stage D (refractory) HFrEF meeting criteria for advanced therapy: EF 15%, VO₂max 9.8 mL/kg/min (threshold <12–14 mL/kg/min), 4+ hospitalisations, maximum GDMT + CRT-D. Cardiac transplantation (Option A) — age 78 is generally above the recommended cutoff (<65–70 years in most centres) and eGFR 28 represents significant renal impairment that may worsen post-transplant with calcineurin inhibitors. LVAD as destination therapy (DT) is appropriate: the REMATCH trial showed LVAD superiority over optimal medical therapy in advanced HF; the MOMENTUM 3 trial (HeartMate 3) showed 2-year survival of 79% with current LVAD technology; age alone is not a contraindication to DT-LVAD if functional status and patient values support it; eGFR 28 (CKD stage 3b) is not an absolute contraindication to LVAD.
whys: Cardiac transplantation — list urgently: Age 78 exceeds typical transplant eligibility criteria (most centres use 65–70 as upper limit, though this is not absolute). More critically, eGFR 28 represents significant CKD — calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus/cyclosporin) post-transplant would further worsen renal function, often requiring dialysis. LVAD DT is the appropriate advanced therapy for this age/renal profile.

Haemodiafiltration for cardiorenal syndrome: While this patient has CKD, haemodiafiltration addresses the consequence (renal failure) not the cause (low cardiac output). It does not treat the underlying Stage D HF and is not a long-term solution. It may be a temporary bridge measure but is not the primary advanced therapy here.

Continuous IV inotrope infusion as palliative bridging: Palliative continuous inotropes (dobutamine, milrinone) are appropriate for end-stage patients who decline or are ineligible for advanced mechanical/transplant therapies, primarily for comfort. However, this patient is ambulatory with good functional status and social support — he is a candidate for LVAD DT. Defaulting to palliation without offering LVAD DT is premature.

Increase furosemide dose and add metolazone: Sequential nephron blockade (furosemide + thiazide-like metolazone) can achieve additional diuresis in diuretic-resistant patients but carries risk of severe electrolyte disturbances and AKI. More importantly, this is a symptom management strategy, not an advanced therapy. This patient requires escalation to mechanical support (LVAD) given Stage D criteria — adjusting diuretics is inadequate.
